results.possibleIdentity: Monroe H. Postman (1928–2012) was a prominent electronic engineer and technical consultant based in the Palo Alto and Los Altos areas of California. He held a Bachelor of Electrical Engineering (BEE) from the Polytechnic Institute of Brooklyn and was a licensed Professional Engineer (PE) in Control Systems. Postman's career was marked by significant contributions to large-scale aerospace and transit projects, as well as a dedicated second career in assistive technology and historical preservation. For approximately 11 years, he worked for Philco-Ford, where he was a lead designer for the printer subsystems of the Philco 2000 (Univac) computer series and contributed to the development of the Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) test track and subsystems. His son also noted his involvement in the Apollo 11 project. Later in his career, Postman served as a Science Advisor and Technology Agent for the City of San Jose under a National Science Foundation (NSF) program, focusing on technology transfer and microcomputer applications in local government. In his later years, he specialized in rehabilitation robotics and mechatronics at the Menlo Park VA Hospital, developing technological solutions for the physically disabled. Beyond his professional work, Postman was a well-known 'idea man' and volunteer in the technical-artistic community; he served as a curator for the Museum of American Heritage in Palo Alto and was an active member of the American Typecasting Fellowship. Notably, he collaborated with artists Clifton and Lois Rather of the Rather Press, inventing electronic speed control devices for their hand-fed printing presses, which aligns with the mission of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).
